lines.text: Psst, I see dead people

annotations.text:
In The Sixth Sense (1999), the film’s protagonist, Cole Sear (potrayed by Haley Joel Osment), can see and communicate with dead people and whispers the same words that the boy utters to his therapist, Malcolm Crowe (potrayed by Bruce Willis) in the movie’s most iconic scene:

https://youtu.be/vSHYy3sgS30?si=HyuG82vjaQjAgM5-

This is also a callback to bars from the final verse of April 2024’s “euphoria,” which ridiculed Drake for using ghostwriters as well as for using artificial intelligence on “Taylor Made Freestyle” which released earlier that month:

Ain’t twenty-v-one, it’s one-v-twenty if I gotta smack niggas that write with you
[…]
Am I battlin' ghost or AI?
Nigga feelin' like Joel Hale Osteen
Funny, he was in a film called A.I.
And my sixth sense tellin' me to off him

In addition, this might be a reference to fellow Compton rapper The Game’s intro on his 50 Cent diss track from July 2006, titled “It’s Okay (One Blood)” which mentions Dr. Dre—who just so happens to be one of Kendrick’s mentors and the person who signed him to Aftermath Entertainment:

Dre, I see dead people

Kendrick may have done so because Drake mentioned Game—or “Chucky T”—on April 2024’s “Family Matters” or because Drake compared himself to 50 Cent on “Push Ups”:

Niggas really got me out here talkin' like I’m 50, ayy

This may also serve as a response to the final lines of Drake’s previous diss track, “Family Matters,” where he repeatedly declared Kendrick was “dead”:

You’re dead
You’re dead, you’re dead
There’s nowhere to hide, there’s nowhere to hide, you know what I mean
They hired a crisis management team to clean up the fact that you beat on your queen
The picture you painted ain’t what it seem, you’re dead

TDE founder Anthony “Top Dawg” Tiffith teased this line via Twitter/X about an hour and a half before the song was uploaded to YouTube:



Kendrick’s use of the “psst” interjection here at the top of the song is reminiscent of the closing bars from the aforementioned “euphoria”:

If you take it there, I’m takin' it further
Psst, that’s something you don’t wanna do

lines.text: (Mustard on the beat, ho)

annotations.text:
Los Angeles-based producer Mustard’s signature producer tag is an excerpt of frequent collaborator and Compton artist YG that originated from YG’s 2011 track “I’m Good.”

This is notable because Drake aligned himself with YG in an attempt to discredit Kendrick’s street cred in his then-previous diss track, “Family Matters”:

You know who really bang a set? My nigga YG

Mustard tweeted the following shortly after the song dropped:

I’ll never turn my back on my city …. and I’m fully loaded

While Mustard shot down the rumor of him sampling Nas‘ “Ether” for the track, the production does feature a sped-up sample from the 1968 track “I Believe To My Soul” by Monk Higgins:

https://youtu.be/UYJ_TSkPSds

lines.text: Ayy, Mustard on the beat, ho

annotations.text:
Los Angeles-based producer Mustard’s signature producer tag is an excerpt of frequent collaborator and Compton artist YG that originated from YG’s 2011 track “I’m Good.”

This is notable because Drake aligned himself with YG in an attempt to discredit Kendrick’s street cred in his then-previous diss track, “Family Matters”:

You know who really bang a set? My nigga YG

Mustard tweeted the following shortly after the song dropped:

I’ll never turn my back on my city …. and I’m fully loaded

While Mustard shot down the rumor of him sampling Nas‘ “Ether” for the track, the production does feature a sped-up sample from the 1968 track “I Believe To My Soul” by Monk Higgins:

https://youtu.be/UYJ_TSkPSds

lines.text: Deebo any rap nigga, he a free throw

annotations.text:
Deebo, portrayed by Compton actor Tommy Lister Jr., is a fictional character from the iconic 1995 film Friday. He is depicted as a sociopathic bully that no one in the community is willing to stand up to. This parallels Kendrick’s depiction of Drake in this song and all the previous installments of his Drake diss tracks. However, here, Kendrick is knowingly taking on the persona of a bully. He may also be making a callback to his verse on “Like That,” where he said, “I’m snatchin' chains,” as, in Friday, Deebo snatches a character’s chain.



Deebo is also the nickname of NBA player DeMar DeRozan, who played for The Chicago Bulls when this track dropped. The significance of this is that Kendrick’s parents also hail from Chicago. Although DeRozan is from Compton, he has a connection to Drake’s hometown, as he previously played for the Toronto Raptors, for whom Drake is an ambassador. Kendrick mentions DeRozan later in the song:

I’m glad DeRoz' came home, y'all didn’t deserve him neither

DeRozan is a proficient free throw shooter, with his 84.1% career average only 6.9% short of the record. Therefore, Kendrick is implying that beefing with other rappers is as effortless for him as free throws are for DeRozan.

DeRozan went on to cameo in the “Not Like Us” visuals.

lines.text: Man down, call an amberlamps, tell him, "Breathe, bro"

annotations.text:
“Man down” is a military term utilized when a soldier is injured during combat. Kendrick likens this to Drake losing the rap battle. “Amberlamps" is a reference to the 2010 AC Transit bus fight, where a man got punched in the face so hard he had difficulty pronouncing the word “ambulance.”

“Amberlamps” may also allude to an AMBER Alert—a notice for endangered, missing, or abducted children. This would coincide with Kendrick’s proclamation that Drake and various members associated with his OVO Sound label are pedophiles, or, as he suggested on May 2024’s “meet the grahams,”:

They be streamlinin' victims all inside of they home and callin' ‘em tender

“Amber Lamps” is also a May 2010 song by Cam’ron, who voiced his opinion and said he favored Drake to Kendrick in a May 2024 appearance on the It Is What It Is podcast.

Kendrick might be threatening to shoot Drake, as “Breathe bro breathe” is the title of a viral TikTok meme where a person is shot and falls to the floor with someone above them repeatedly saying “Breathe!” This could also refer to a famous Drake meme:

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5PM2DnndxcQ

lines.text: Nail a nigga to the cross, he walk around like Teezo

annotations.text:
Kendrick likens his verbal annihilation of Drake to a crucifixion, which traditionally involves the nailing or fastening of an individual to a cross — two perpendicular blocks of wood. The line could also be a double entendre as a cross can be symbolized as a “T”:

Nail a nigga to the cross, he walk around like TEEZO/T, so

Rapper-singer Teezo Touchdown famously wears nails in his hair. He notably collaborated with Drake on the songs “Amen” and “7969 Santa” from October 2023’s For All The Dogs.



Additionally, “Teezo” is a homophone of Tissot, a Swiss watch company whose logo features their national flag—a cross.

lines.text: What's up with these jabroni-ass niggas tryna see Compton?

annotations.text:
Born and raised in Compton, Lamar is pointing out his frustration with Drake’s constant antagonizations that he isn’t scared of coming to his hometown.

Drake has mentioned that he gets more love than Kendrick does in Compton on “Push Ups”:

 Get more love in the city that you from, nigga

Shortly after taking down his Lamar diss track “Taylor Made Freestyle,” Drake took to Instagram to post a photo where he was rocking a Compton Community College Shirt. The college was allegedly attended by Lamar’s fiancée, Whitney Alford.



But this goes back a long way; Back in 2015, Drake made a guest appearance on The Game’s song “100.” They even filmed the music video in Compton, where The Game is from. In the video, the Compton based rapper is showing Drake around his hometown. But here’s where it gets interesting—on that track, Drake threw a bit of a sneaky jab at Kendrick Lamar:

I would have all of your fans
If I didn’t go pop and I stayed on some conscious shit



Lamar refers to Drake as a “jabroni”, which is a wrestling term invented by The Iron Sheik and popularized by The Rock. Jabroni has multiple meanings, including a stupid person or a “jobber”, and in the context of wrestling it refers to an enhancement talent. The sole purpose of a jobber is to lose to established wrestlers while making them look good in the process, thereby enhancing their popularity. Lamar is saying that he doesn’t see Drake as serious competition but rather an easy win, likening the result of their feud to that of a predetermined match in wrestling.

The word “jabroni” was also popularized in modern slang by the show It’s Always Sunny in Philadelphia by the character Mac when referring to somebody with zero culture awareness or poor understanding of the city, which fits with how Lamar keeps depicting Drake as a “Culture Vulture”.

lines.text: The industry can hate me, fuck 'em all and they mama

annotations.text:
Kendrick unapologetically declares his intention to confront Drake, regardless of industry alliances. He acknowledges the potential repercussions, but doubles down on sentiments conveyed on April 2024’s “euphoria”:

Whoever that’s fuckin' with him, fuck you niggas, and fuck the industry too

On “6:16 in LA,” Kendrick elaborates on this mindset, portraying his choice as a catalyst for personal development amid adversity:

God, ah, my confession is yours, but
Who am I if I don’t go to war?
There’s opportunity when livin' with loss
I discover myself when I fall short
But war-ready if the world is ready to see you bleed

Kendrick is also referencing one of the most memorable diss tracks of all time, June 1996’s “Hit ’Em Up” by 2Pac:

Fuck you and yo' motherfuckin' mama!

The word “opps” could refer to opponents or enemies. Kendrick might be say that he will criticize Drake and and will not care what music industry thinks about me

lines.text: How many opps you really got? I mean, it's too many options
I'm finna pass on this body, I'm John Stockton

annotations.text:
John Stockton is a legendary basketballer for Utah Jazz in the NBA who holds the record for most assists in NBA history with 15,806.



Stockton famously played much of his career alongside power forward Karl Malone, hence his reputation as a passer. Malone, however, is notorious for impregnating a 13-year-old girl in 1983 at age 20.

Kendrick is playing into the rumors of pedophilia surrounding Drake, while also suggesting that he can skillfully move past this moment if he chooses; passing the proverbial ball to any of Drake’s number of enemies.

lines.text: Beat your ass and hide the Bible if God watchin'

annotations.text:
K-Dot makes it clear that he will put aside his well-established Christian beliefs and values if he has to hurt Drake or anyone else.

This bar bears resemblance to some wordplay found on April 2017’s “ELEMENT.”—another track which was directed at Drake:

Put the Bible down and go eye for an eye for this shit

This bar also alludes to the term “bible thumper," referring to people who aggressively spread their religious beliefs to others.

lines.text: Sometimes you gotta pop out and show niggas

annotations.text:
Kendrick calls back to a line from Drake’s April 2024 diss track, “Taylor Made Freestyle,” rapped from the perspective of an AI-generated Tupac Shakur:

You supposed to be the boogeyman, go do what you do

The idea of being a “boogeyman” alludes to the fact that no one would want to go up against Kendrick but, at the time, Drake used the label to suggest that he was ready, willing, able, and unafraid. The mention of a “certified boogeyman” here connects to the previous line where Kendrick says he is going to “pop out,“ as well as playing on Drake’s September 2021 album Certified Lover Boy.

Upping “the score” likely refers to the fact that Kendrick has double dropped after Drake’s response but, interestingly, the line may also refer to the key signature of the track—while “meet the grahams” was indeed in “A minor,” “Not Like Us” is in B minor. The phrase may also coincide with gang lingo, suggesting Kendrick is counting his kills.

lines.text: Certified boogeyman, I'm the one that up the score with 'em
Walk him down, whole time, I know he got some ho in him

annotations.text:
In street slang, “walking down” refers to approaching an enemy on foot before shooting them, as opposed to shooting at them while driving by in a car. In boxing, walking down an opponent refers to a tactic in which a fighter constantly applies pressure by moving towards an opponent that is trying to maintain distance. Kendrick implies that even though Drake is reeling from Kendrick’s verbal onslaught, he won’t let Drake run away from the attack. The line is a callback to “euphoria,” in which Kendrick describes walking down his enemy.

In the second half of the line, Kendrick is once again calling attention to his ability to predict Drake’s actions. The term “ho” contributes to the image of Drake being fearful, while also implying things like promiscuity, femininity, untrustworthiness, and a lack of morals.

lines.text: Pole on him, extort shit, bully Death Row on him

annotations.text:
On the surface, death row refers to the place where criminals await execution or capital punishment. However, here, it doubles as a nod to Death Row Records, the infamous label founded by the ever-intimidating Suge Knight, who signed 2Pac in 1995. Incidentally, as of a 2022 acquisition, it is owned and operated by Snoop Dogg—who signed to the label in 1991.



After Drake and his team of producers utilized A.I. technology to emulate 2Pac and Snoop Dogg on his April 2024 diss track, “Taylor Made Freestyle,” Snoop could be seen wearing a Death Row tracksuit in his video response on Instagram.

To “pull” on someone refers to the act of brandishing a firearm, which is also colloquially referred to as a “pole,” while the mention of extortion may double as a broad claim being made against Drake and his OVO Sound label as well as a subtle callback to a line from Kendrick’s first diss track— April 2024’s “euphoria”:

Ayy, Top Dawg, who the fuck they think they playin' with?
Extortion my middle name as soon as you jump off of that plane, bitch

It’s also worth noting that “bully Death Row on him” sounds similar to “bully the flow on him,” which is precisely what Kendrick intends to do with the diss tracks that stemmed from his and Drake’s well-documented beef.

lines.text: Say, Drake, I hear you like 'em young

annotations.text:
Kendrick directly interpolates an old Drake tweet from 2009 where he declares that he’s sleeping with someone so young he should be going to jail.



Kendrick continues the accusations of Drake’s connections with younger women.

Back in 2018, there was a lot of discussion about Drake and actress Millie Bobby Brown’s friendship, which sparked interest due to their age gap. This all started when Millie, just 14 at the time, met Drake at his concert in Brisbane and posted about it on Instagram, calling him “fantastic” and “a great friend.” Their close bond raised questions with some questioning the appropriateness of their relationship given Drake’s way older age and reputation.



Also, back in 2010, there was a video circulating around where Drake started kissing a 17-year-old in one of his concerts and after finding out her age he said:

Seventeen? Why you look like that? You thick, look at all this. I don’t know if I should feel guilty or not, but I had fun. I like the way your breasts feel against my chest.

Lastly, Drake previously dated a model named Bella Harris when she was 18. However, Drake himself made posts on social media that indicated he had relations with her when she was underage. Drake was 30 years old when the post from 2016 was made, which means Harris was 16.

lines.text: You better not ever go to cell block one

annotations.text:
Doubling-down on the sexual misconduct allegations, Kendrick is insinuating that Drake should be imprisoned as a result.

The prison system can be a menacing place for child molesters/sexual offenders. This could be Kendrick alluding to the known fact that sexual offenders are more likely to be murdered than any other inmate demographic.

Kendrick already expressed as much on his earlier release “​meet the grahams”:

Him and Weinstein should get fucked up in a cell for the rest they life

lines.text: To any bitch that talk to him and they in love

annotations.text:
Kendrick doubles down on calling Drake a pedophile, this time warning his potential lovers.

Drake has stirred controversy over his involvement with underage girls; a video has even surfaced of him kissing a 17-year-old girl onstage at a concert back in 2010.

Kendrick’s warning echoes lines from his previous Drake diss track, “meet the grahams”:

To any woman that be playin' his music, know that you’re playin' your sister
Or better, you’re sellin' your niece, to the weirdos, not the good ones

These lines serve as a warning that if you allow Drake near your female family relatives, then you’re putting them in danger.

lines.text: Just make sure you hide your lil' sister from him
They tell me Chubbs the only one that get your hand-me-downs

annotations.text:
This line is based upon two differing usages of the phrase “hand-me-downs.” Literally, a “hand-me-down” refers to a possession that is no longer wanted or needed and this is offered to to someone close to you, like a family member or friend. The second usage plays upon the first, but draws upon the specific work that Chubbs performs for Drake.

Chubbs is a key member of Drake’s OVO, supposedly as OVO’s head of security, Within the context of the last line, Kendrick seems to  imply that Chubbs is the one Drake is giving family-like charity to, whereas he only has other, likely sexual, interests in the younger family members of the women he sleeps with, rather than being interested in helping or nurturing them.

This would similarly suggest that Drake has few genuine relationships, implying that Drake has not given his son, Adonis, his “hand-me-downs,” but instead has choosen to give them to Chubbs. Chubbs, being on Drake’s payroll, and believed to only be friends with Drake due to money and fame, would conceivably have a weaker relationship with Drake than Drake would have with his son.

By saying that only Chubbs receives Drakes hand-me-downs, Kendrick is implying that Drake’s relationship with his family and son is weaker than his relationship with a disingenuous friend like Chubbs.

The second usage of “hand-me-downs” refers to the literal work Chubbs actually performs for Drake when driving around and looking for attractive women, interviewing them, and then selecting the best ones to bring back to Drake to have sex with. TikToker @eileensgarden went viral in October 2021 for exposing Drake’s alleged “private Tinder”:

Everything I’m about to tell you is a hundred percent true. And this information came to me from one of the crew members on Drake’s tour. This was the guy who did all the tech stuff. And he told me that Drake has an employee whose job is to go around town looking for super hot girls. He finds the girls and then he interviews them, and out of all the girls he interviews, he picks the one that he thinks is the best. Then, he drives her to Drake’s place, Drake has sex with her, and then he drives her home. So, basically, Drake has his own private Tinder.

This is further demonstrated by Drake’s own lyrics in “Chicago Freestyle”:

All good, Chubbs will look around
Find one that seem my type
That my dawg and he know what I like
He done found me plenty in my life

From this it appears that  the “hand-me-downs” refer to allowing Chubbs to have sex with the same women that Drake brings home to have sex with since Drake is now no longer interested in them.

lines.text: And Party at the party playin' with his nose now

annotations.text:
Kendrick mocks the drug use—specifically cocaine—of OVO singer and songwriter PARTYNEXTDOOR. He was arrested in 2017 while attempting to cross the US-Canada border in possession of Xanax and Oxycodone.



 PND’s mugshot taken after the arrest 

Fans and others have speculated for years that he has a severe issue with substance abuse.

lines.text: And Baka got a weird case, why is he around?

annotations.text:
Travis Savoury, known as Baka Not Nice, was previously a former security guard who signed with Drake’s record label, OVO Sound, an imprint of Warner Records from 2017.

In 2014, Baka was arrested and charged with forcing a 22-year-old woman into prostitution and taking her money. Baka’s trafficking case was dismissed after she refused to testify and was instead convicted of assaulting her. This led to Drake mentioning Baka’s release from jail on the 2015 track, “Know Yourself”:

I might declare it a holiday as soon as Baka get back on the road

Drake also said:

When Baka got out of jail there was a lot of different paths he could’ve took and he decided to focus on music.

Drake and Baka

Kendrick questions Drake’s decision to keep Baka around, which goes with his other accusations that Drake is a sex offender.

lines.text: Certified Lover Boy? Certified pedophiles

annotations.text:
Certified Lover Boy is the title of Drake’s sixth studio album, which he released on September 3, 2021. His use of the word “pedophile” stems from Drake’s history of repeatedly allegedly grooming and showing interest in teenage girls. For example, back in 2010, there was a video circulating where a then-23-year-old Drake started kissing a 17-year-old girl at one of his concerts. After finding out her age, he kissed her again and said:

I can’t go to jail yet, man! You seventeen? Why you look like that? You thick, look at all this. I don’t know if I should feel guilty or not, but I had fun. I like the way your breasts feel against my chest.

Here, however, Kendrick is also seemingly referring to members of Drake’s entourage as sex offenders. For example, Travis Savoury, known as “Baka Not Nice,” was a former security guard who signed with Drake’s OVO Sound record label in 2017. In 2014, Baka was arrested and charged with assault and human trafficking. This adds weight to Kendrick’s use of the phrase “lover boy,” a term synonymous with “romeo pimps” in police lexicon; these criminals prey on young girls, making them fall in love before exploiting them for profit.

Kendrick previously claimed Drake’s entourage was filled with sex offenders on May 2024’s “meet the grahams”:

He got sex offenders on ho-VO that he keep on a monthly allowance



The cover art for “Not Like Us” features an image of Drake’s mansion edited in a way that closely mimics the modern sex offender registry, pinpointing the residency of a convicted offender. Notably, there are 13 of them represented as pins inside Drake’s mansion, again suggesting that Drake’s whole clique is guilty. It’s also worth pointing out the way Kendrick pronounces the word “pedophiles”—sounding eerily similar to pediph-owls—as the OVO mascot is an owl, where the Os are meant to look like owl eyes and the V looks like a beak.

lines.text: Wop, wop, wop, wop, wop, Dot, fuck 'em up

annotations.text:
The “wop” onomatopoeia mimics the sound of a flurry of punches thrown in quick succession. Kendrick is overwhelming Drake with his lyrical jabs. The adlib delivery mirrors the 2010 club hit “Wop” by Florida rapper J. Dash. Dot is short for K-Dot, Kendrick’s nickname and original rap alias.

Kendrick’s “lyrical jabs” in the official MV.

lines.text: Wop, wop, wop, wop, wop, I'ma do my stuff
Why you trollin' like a bitch? Ain't you tired?

annotations.text:
To “strike a chord” means to affect or stir someone’s emotions. This is likely a reference to Drake posting memes about Kendrick throughout their feud, but may also refer to his April 2024 diss track. On “Taylor Made Freestyle” the Canadian rapper used an A.I. generated vocal of Kendrick’s idol, Tupac Shakur:

Kendrick, we need ya, the West Coast savior

Kendrick uses the musical chord of “A-Minor” as a double entendre, since the homophone “a minor” coincides with numerous accusations made against Drake regarding inappropriate behavior with underage girls. K-Dot mentions this earlier in the verse:

Say, Drake, I hear you like ‘em young

Incidentally, part of Drake’s May 2024 diss track, “Family Matters,” is in the key of A-Minor. Much like that part of that track, Kendrick draws out the final word of the bar, just as Drake did to highlight his line about Kendrick’s business partner and PgLang co-founder Dave Free:

I heard that one of em' little kids might be Dave Free’s

Lastly, it’s worth noting that the A-Minor scale doesn’t utilize any black keys on a piano—only white ones. This could be a deeper criticism directed at Drake about his biracial background.

 Kendrick dances over hopscotch lines during this moment in the music video.

lines.text: Tryna strike a chord and it's probably A minor
They not like us, they not like us, they not like us

annotations.text:
Kendrick comments on how Drake (and the people he surrounds himself with) are “not like us,” saying that anybody that Kendrick surrounds himself with would not replicate the behaviours that he refers to within the track, a contrast of pgLang and Top Dawg Entertainment with OVO Sound in terms of moral fibre, values and its ability to fit into Black American culture.

This theme of racial consideration has also likely been directed at Drake’s biracial background and the issues of acceptance that come with it, both from himself or from others. This subject has come up multiple times in Drake’s career, on his own tracks, such as November 2023’s “Wick Man”:

Black America love to remind me what my mama look like
As if I’d ever fuckin' forget
I’m never enough

And on other people’s songs too, such as Pusha T’s 2018 diss track “The Story of Adidon”:

Confused, always felt you weren’t Black enough
Afraid to grow it ‘cause your ‘fro wouldn’t nap enough

This can also be seen throughout the plethora of recent diss tracks between Drake and his opponents, including on Rick Ross’s “Champagne Moments”, where he would rap:

White boy, I see you, I see you

In reference to his White Jewish mother’s ethnic heritage and his suburban upbringing, linking back to the line with Kendrick pointing out that Drake’s childhood was unlike many of Black Americans that he attempts to fit in with. Alongside this, we can also analyse the track’s cover art, which sees an unknown White man’s yearbook photo that quickly became a meme in 2022 after it was identified that he comically resembled the Canadian rapper.



Above: the aforementioned cover art of Rick Ross's Champagne Moments


and Kendrick Lamar’s “euphoria”, on which he would state:

I even hate when you say the word “nigga,” but that’s just me, I guess
Some shit just cringeworthy, it ain’t even gotta be deep, I guess
[…]
We don’t wanna hear you say “nigga” no more
We don’t wanna hear you say “nigga” no more
Stop

To even further increase the gap between the two, highlighting how he feels uncomfortable with his usage of the N-word as he doesn’t acknowledge him as a Black person.

It is also important to note that Kendrick and Drake’s careers took off at around the same time. “They not like us” may allude to Kendrick’s commitment to authenticity and lyricism (the heart and soul of hip hop), in contrast to Drake’s catchy yet vapid songs. Here, Kendrick continues to differentiate himself from Drake not only on the basis of race, but also in terms of true commitment to the craft and its Black origins.

Finally, in “Kendrick Lamar’s Inner Drive,” a Lamar interview with SZA in the October 21, 2024 Harper’s Bazaar, SZA engages this song directly, asking “What does ‘Not Like Us’ mean to you?,” to which Lamar responds:

Not like us? Not like us is the energy of who I am, the type of man I represent. Now, if you identify with the man that I represent […] This man has morals, he has values, he believes in something, he stands on something. He’s not pandering. He’s a man who can recognize his mistakes and not be afraid to share the mistakes and can dig deep down into fear-based ideologies or experiences to be able to express them without feeling like he’s less of a man. If I’m thinking of ‘Not Like Us,’ I’m thinking of me and whoever identifies with that.

lines.text: They not like us, they not like us, they not like us
You think the Bay gon' let you disrespect Pac, nigga?

annotations.text:
In the first line, Kendrick criticizes Drake for mimicking deceased rapper 2Pac’s voice using artificial intelligence on his second Kendrick diss track “Taylor Made Freestyle.” Less than a week after the track dropped, 2Pac’s estate sent Drake a cease-and-desist letter demanding he “remove it from all platforms,” stating they were “deeply dismayed” by his actions; he complied.

On his follow-up diss track “Family Matters,” Drake made the following allegation against Kendrick:

A cease-and-desist is for hoes, can’t listen to lies that come out of your mouth
You called the Tupac estate and begged ‘em to sue me and get that shit down

So it seems Kendrick is responding to Drake by saying he had no part in the situation but that Drake should’ve known using 2Pac’s voice wasn’t going to be ignored either way.

Tupac Shakur was born in New York and went to high school in Baltimore before he moved to Northern California’s San Francisco Bay Area. He lived in Marin City and Oakland in the late ‘80s and early '90s before he connected with Shock G’s group Digital Underground, who put him on his first song, “Same Song,” and helped him release his debut album, 2Pacalypse Now, both in 1991.

2Pac’s ties with Oakland and The Bay started with his parents' involvement with the Black Panther Party, which was founded in Oakland in 1966 as a group to police the police and support Black communities. 2Pac was also connected with some of the Bay Area’s biggest rappers, including E-40, MC Hammer, and Too $hort while being revered by many more who might be willing to have Drake dealt with at a show in The Town — potentially making that his “last stop” — for playing with Pac’s legacy. On November 3, 2023, a stretch of MacArthur Boulevard in Oakland was named “Tupac Shakur Way” in his honor.

lines.text: I think that Oakland show gon' be your last stop, nigga
Did Cole foul, I don't know why you still pretendin'

annotations.text:
Kendrick may be referring to three things here:


He’s saying Drake did J. Cole dirty by bringing him onto his song, “First Person Shooter” and allowing him to think that he, Drake, and Kendrick were on equal standing with each other. After Kendrick’s response to this claim on “Like That,” Cole responded with “7 Minute Drill” then immediately apologized to Kendrick and bowed out of the beef. Kendrick suggests that Cole did the right thing and knew his place, and is wondering why Drake still thinks he’s capable of taking on Kendrick instead of following Cole’s footsteps.
Kendrick is being less generous to J. Cole here, suggesting that he is the one who did Cole foul by dissing him on “Like That,” causing a chain of humiliating events for Cole, including Cole responding with a rap about having shooters, losing sleep over his own diss, apologizing to Kendrick, exiting the beef, and deleting “7 Minute Drill” off of streaming sites. Again, he would be wondering why Drake still thinks he’s capable of taking on Kendrick after all of this.
Drake throwing J. Cole under the bus after being on each other’s side, portraying him negatively in “Push Ups” and “Family Matters.”



In “Push Ups,” Drake says

I don’t care what Cole think, that Dot shit was weak as fuck

In “Family Matters,” Drake says

And, nigga, Cole losin' sleep on this, it ain’t me

and

You right about “Fuck the big three,” it’s only Big D, and there’s video proof

Both lines reinforces Drake’s willingness to take on Kendrick while painting J.Cole as weak.

Also “foul” is a homophone for fowl which leads into the subsequent bird lines.

lines.text: What is the owl? Bird niggas and burnt bitches, go

annotations.text:
Drake is the founder and owner of OVO Sound record label. OVO stands for October’s Very Own and its logo is an owl, as a nod to the way the letters “OVO” resemble an owl’s face. Kendrick plays on this by insulting everyone associated with OVO. “Bird” is an insult meaning someone who’s unintelligent and an embarrassment.

 

“OVO” Owl logo

At the end of the Not Like Us music video, there is an owl locked in a cage.

lines.text: The audience not dumb

annotations.text:
Drake has been trying to use his fame and larger fan base to shape the narrative around this conflict. For instance, Lamar alleged that Drake tried to cease and desist “Like That” in the lyrics of “euphoria”:

Try cease and desist on the “Like That” record?
Ho, what? You ain’t like that record?

Drake denied the allegation in his diss track “Family Matters”:

A cease and desist is for hoes, can’t listen to lies that come out of your mouth
You called the Tupac estate and begged ‘em to sue me and get that shit down

In a post after “Not Like Us” was released, Metro Boomin' to confirmed Lamar’s claims with a screenshot from an email regarding the cease and desist action:




However, even before Metro’s confirmation, some screenshots were published on the ktt2 forum of an alleged cease and desist action.

Kendrick is informing Drake that his days of misleading the broader public are coming to a close with the new revelations that are coming out in this diss war.

lines.text: Shape the stories how you want, hey, Drake, they're not slow
Rabbit hole is still deep, I can go further, I promise

annotations.text: “Going down a rabbit hole” is to find oneself in a situation that one has spent too much time and effort in and can’t get out of. Kendrick is claiming that there is even more to the situation about Drake than what he’s revealed thus far.

lines.text: Ain't that somethin'? B-Rad stands for bitch and you Malibu most wanted

annotations.text:
Kendrick references the movie Malibu’s Most Wanted, likening Drake to the main character Brad Gluckman, who attempts to emulate Black culture by becoming a rapper under the name “B-Rad”.  Kendrick plays on this name in order to call Drake a “bitch”. With this, Kendrick furthers his narrative that Drake is an outsider to the culture of hip hop and uses the impression of blackness to further his rap career and resolve his insecurities about his biracial identity.

Notably, Malibu is a popular city in California known for its affluence, suburban lifestyle, and celebrity. In the second half of this line, Kendrick teases that Drake belongs in a place like Malibu, as opposed to the gritty environment of a West Coast hip hop staple like Compton.

B-Rad is also Jewish. He had a bar mitzvah as a kid, and would play rap to be rebellious, much like Drake.



B-Rad is a parody of Eminem’s white rapper character in 8 Mile, B-Rabbit. The ‘Rabbit hole’ of the previous line is revealed here to have a double meaning of being a hole full of Rabbits, e.g.  white rappers like B-Rad and Drake.

lines.text: Ain't no law, boy, you ball boy, fetch Gatorade or somethin'

annotations.text:
Kendrick Lamar is suggesting that Drake doesn’t hold any real authority or power. Instead, he refers to Drake as a “ball boy,” someone who typically assists athletes by fetching items like Gatorade during breaks in a game.

Further, this could refer to Drake’s loyalties in sports. Drake is constantly spotted court side or field side for a variety of different teams and is notorious for being a bandwagon. Perhaps Kendrick is mocking Drake for his obsession with sports without ability to play.

lines.text: Since 2009, I had this bitch jumpin'

annotations.text:
Kendrick Lamar officially dropped the K-Dot moniker in 2009, upon the release of his self-titled EP.



Ironically, on the tape’s final track, “Determined,” Kendrick says:

She listenin' to Drake and all I can say is, ‘Damn
These niggas that much better than me, baby?’

Here, he employs some more bully wordplay in the vein of the “Deebo” reference found during the opening bars. This may be a rebuttal to Drake implying that he made K-Dot’s career by taking him on tour in 2012, but it may also be a subtle nod to one of Kendrick’s early singles, “Bitch I’m In The Club,” from January 2009.

lines.text: You niggas'll get a wedgie, be flipped over your boxers
What OVO for? The "Other Vaginal Option"? Pussy

annotations.text:
Kendrick pokes fun at Drake’s record label, October’s Very Own (sometimes referred to as OVO Sound), in a similar manner  to one of his previous diss tracks, “euphoria”:

Yeah, OV-ho niggas is dick riders

“The Other Vaginal Option” might be a homosexual accusation towards Drake, stating that instead of having vaginal sex, he has anal sex with other men.

In the very last word of the line, Kendrick has a double entendre. He uses the word “pussy,” both as a reference to the “other vaginal option,” and as an insult to Drake, calling him one.

lines.text: Nigga better straighten they posture, got famous all up in Compton

annotations.text:
This line may be a dig at Drake’s producer and fellow crew member, OVO 40.

OVO 40 is diagnosed with Multiple Sclerosis (MS), an autoimmune disease that affects the central nervous system. MS is known to cause nerve damage and affect muscles throughout the body.

Due to the damage to the CNS, it is common for someone with MS to have serious issues with their posture as the disease progresses, and this can leave them hunched over. Kendrick is literally and figuratively telling OVO 40 to “straighten up his posture.” This is playing off of the line “flipped over your boxers.”

Pusha-T used a similar line directed at 40 and his condition on his 2018 Drake diss track, “The Story of Adidon”:

OVO 40 hunched over like he 80

It’s also important to note Kendrick’s play on the words “for/four” in the previous line, setting up the jab for the next bar.
“What’s OVO (4)?”

lines.text: Might write this for the doctorate, tell the pop star quit hidin'

annotations.text:
At the start of the beef after Kendrick was featured on “Like That”, Drake, the “POPSTAR” was the one trying to corral a response from Kendrick on “Push Ups” and “Taylor Made Freestyle”.

You tryna let this shit die down, nah, nah, nah
Not this time, nigga, you followin' through

Now that Kendrick has responded in the form of “euphoria”, “6:16 in LA”, and “Meet the Grahams”, Kendrick is eagerly awaiting for Drake’s response to his various allegations against him.

In “Family Matters” Drake suggested that he’s going on vacation, and that the beef was over and called in his favor. He’s also been long awaiting this vacation.

I’m goin' on vacation now, hope next time, y'all plan it right

After dropping For All The Dogs Scary Hours Edition, he mentioned on “Stories About My Brother”, that he walked back in the studio, unable to leave the rap game alone.

I walked in the studio, Noel said, “I thought you quit”
I said “Nah, I’m quittin' again ‘cause I started again”
Gotta wait on that
This like the storm before the calm, we’ll get to the vacation later

Kendrick is taunting Drake telling him to stop posting on Instagram, stop writing captions, and hop back in the action.

At the time of release, Drake’s only response to “Meet the Grahams” had been his Instagram story of him asking for people to find his supposed hidden daughter.

You lied about your son, you lied about your daughter, huh

lines.text: Fuck a caption, want action, no accident
And I'm hands-on, he fuck around, get polished

annotations.text:
Kendrick Lamar is continuing with a theme iterated on April 2024’s “euphoria,” when he said:

If you take it there, I’m takin' it further
Psst, that’s something you don’t wanna do

For example, Kendrick released multiple tracks in quick succession to one-up Drake’s response. Here, he uses the term “polished”—as in “killed”—while also potentially referring to Drake’s recent infatuation with getting his nails painted; this is widely attributed to his association with rappers like Lil Yachty.



Drake first showcased the look during a stream for his betting company, Stake, and was immediately clowned by the hip-hop world. He also showed off a pink nail job in an Instagram story the night before his 2023 It’s All A Blur Tour.

Additionally, it may refer back to a line on Drake’s April 2024 diss track, “Push Ups”:

Drizzy Chip ‘n Dale, probably got your bitch Chanel
I just got 'em done, boy, don’t make me have to chip a nail

lines.text: Fucked on Wayne girl while he was in jail, that's connivin'

annotations.text:
While Lil Wayne—Drake’s mentor and then-label head—was imprisoned in 2010, Drake confessed to Wayne that he had slept with his then-partner, model/actress Tammy Torres.

 Tammy photographed separately with Wayne and Drake

Wayne touched on this betrayal in his 2010 and 2011 tracks “6 Foot 7 Foot” and “How To Hate”, but explored his feelings in-depth in his prison diary, published in 2016 as Gone ‘Til November: A Journal of Rikers Island. The following excerpt circulated in 2015:


I woke up still feeling fucked up about the fucked-up day that I had. Hell is what it was! I’m used to arguing with my girl [daily], but finding out that she fucked Drake was the absolute worst thing I could’ve found out.

As a man, honestly, that shit hurt, and not because it was Drake. It could’ve been any man, and it would’ve hurt the same. She said it happened way before we got together, but she just never told me. When Drizzy came to see me, he was like, ‘Yeah, it’s true … don’t fuck with her like that ’cause I did fuck her.’ Damn!

This is the type of shit that a man never wants to find out while he’s locked up. Or maybe so, ’cause only God knows what I would’ve done if I wasn’t locked up right now. As soon as I found out, I told them to just lock me in, and I’ve basically just been by myself in my cell for the last couple of days. […] As a man, I’ll admit it: That shit really fucked me up.


From this, Kendrick appears to be misreporting the timeline, whether intentionally or not. Alternatively, he could be inferring that Tammy lied about when she slept with Drake.

Kendrick references Drake getting a tattoo of Wayne’s face on his left tricep in 2017, approximately two years after the above diary excerpt surfaced. Kendrick implies that paying tribute in this way is insufficient given the part Drake played in hurting his mentor.

Kendrick may also be referencing Drake’s 2023 face tattoo above his right eyebrow that reads “miskeen”. The term is supposedly Toronto slang for “sweet and innocent”, but it’s a word in Arabic that translates to “poor and pathetic”. Given Kendrick’s primarily serving to discredit Drake by exposing him as an alleged pedophile, Kendrick could be playing off both these meanings, namely Drake’s “poor and pathetic” for targeting the “sweet and innocent”.

lines.text: Then get his face tatted like a bitch apologizin'
I'm glad DeRoz' came home, y'all didn't deserve him neither

annotations.text:
Kendrick refers to DeMar DeRozan, also known as “De-Roz,” who is a professional basketball player and 6-time NBA all-star from Compton, California, Kendrick’s hometown. DeRozan played for Canada’s lone NBA team, the Toronto Raptors, a team which Drake is the Global Ambassador for. Kendrick suggests that De-Roz is an amazing player, inasmuch as the Raptors did not fully appreciate his abilities. DeRozan was traded from the Raptors to the San Antonio Spurs in 2018, DeRozan now plays for the Sacramento Kings, in his home state of California.



On June 19, 2024, Kendrick bought out DeRozan while performing “Not Like Us” at his concert “The Pop Out: Ken & Friends”:

https://twitter.com/nfr_podcast/status/1803621191296377152?ref_src=twsrc%5Egoogle%7Ctwcamp%5Eserp%7Ctwgr%5Etweet

On July 4th, 2024, the “Not Like Us” music video was released. DeMar DeRozan makes a cameo in the video when Kendrick raps this line.

https://youtu.be/H58vbez_m4E?si=EYw6IYkIcLPSZiRj&t=162

lines.text: From Alondra down to Central, nigga better not speak on Serena

annotations.text:
Kendrick warns Drake to not disrespect Serena Williams again, particularly in Los Angeles, as like Kendrick she was raised in Compton. Alondra Boulevard is a west–east thoroughfare and Central Avenue is a north–south thoroughfare, which intersect in Compton next to Tragniew Park, where Venus and Serena played tennis in their youth.

Drake and Serena were reportedly romantically involved in 2015 according to TMZ Sports, where photos surfaced of them spotted kissing in a restaurant:

Drake and Serena Kissing

In 2022, Drake name dropped Serena on track “Middle Of The Ocean”, where Drake disses Serena’s husband, calling him a groupie:

Sidebar, Serena, your husband a groupie
He claim we don’t got a problem but
No, boo, it is like you comin’ for sushi
We might pop up on ’em at will like Suzuki

As we have learnt in this feud, Kendrick has zero tolerance for disrespecting family, so Drake disrespecting Serena’s husband may be the reason Kendrick has stepped in to defend her. As Kendrick rapped on previous Drake diss track “meet the grahams”:

But you fucked up the moment you called out my family’s name

Serena and Kendrick

lines.text: And your homeboy need subpoena, that predator move in flocks

annotations.text:
Lamar doubles down on his allegation that Drake is a pedophile. Sex offenders are, via Megan’s Law, legally required to be publicly identifiable to their local community in the U.S. This line embodies the cover art of the song, as it displays a Google Earth screenshot of Drake’s $100 million dollar Toronto mansion photoshopped onto a sex offender app GPS with various pings over the estate:



The mansion, dubbed “The Embassy” by Drake, was the exact estate he suggested would be raided for sex crimes in his May 2024 diss track “meet the grahams:”

The Embassy ‘bout to get raided, too, it’s only a matter of time

Prior to that, Kendrick called Drake a predator and implied that Drake ran a sex trafficking ring of sorts in his home:

And we gotta raise our daughters knowin' there’s predators like him lurkin'
[…]
It’s some weird shit goin' on and some of these artists be here to police it
They be streamlinin' victims all inside of they home and callin' ‘em tender
Then leak videos of themselves to further push their agendas

The homeboy needing subpoena likely refers to OVO signee Baka Not Nice, who was arrested for sex trafficking and assault in June 2014.

The word “flocks” could be another reference to birds, as a group of birds is often called a “flock.” This is in addition to previous lines in the song where Kendrick uses the word “foul” as a homophone for fowl:

Did Cole foul, I don’t know why you still pretendin’

He also compares members of OVO, whose logo is an owl, to birds:

What is the owl? Bird niggas and bird bitches, go

This is subtly similar to a diss Megan Thee Stallion made in her 2024 diss track “HISS” where she invoked Megan’s Law:

These hoes don’t be mad at Megan, these hoes mad at Megan’s Law

While the diss was widely assumed  to be aimed at Nicki Minaj, some believed the track could also be aimed at Drake. The two had a public feud in 2022 when Drake seemed to make light of her widely publicized 2020 shooting in a punchline. It should be noted that Kendrick is fond of Megan, as he shouted her out a while back on August 2021’s “family ties:”

Show my ass and take y'all to class
I can multitask like Megan, brother

lines.text: That name gotta be registered and placed on neighborhood watch
I lean on you niggas like another line of Wock'

annotations.text:
Lean is a recreational drug made out of cough syrup, namely the Wockhardt brand.



In the same way that Wockhardt cough syrup is literally in lean, Kendrick “leans on” or is propped up by the broader hip-hop sphere, hence the following line. This was a point of emphasis on his April 2024 diss track “euphoria”:

And notice, I said ‘we,’ it’s not just me, I’m what the culture feelin'

Also, for this line and the next line, Lamar gradually raises his tone and pitch for the beat drop which lasts for the rest of the verse.

lines.text: Yeah, it's all eyes on me, and I'ma send it up to Pac, ayy

annotations.text:
Kendrick acknowledges that he’s in the spotlight, while making a nod to February 1996’s All Eyez On Me by 2Pac. Many, including Kendrick, found it distasteful when Drake replicated the late rapper’s likeness using A.I. on his April 2024 diss track, “Taylor Made Freestyle”—Tupac Shakur’s Estate went so far as to send a cease and desist letter in response. Kendrick indicted Drake for this as well on April 2024’s “euphoria”:

Somebody had told me that you got a ring, on God, I’m ready to double the wage
I’d rather do that than let a Canadian nigga make Pac turn in his grave

“Put the wrong label on me, I'ma get ‘em dropped” can have several interpretations. Firstly, it refers to the fact that Drake accused Kendrick of engaging in domestic violence on his own May 2024 diss track, “Family Matters.” Secondly, Kendrick is not happy about these accusations and his revenge will come in whatever form necessary, whether verbal or physical. Thirdly, getting dropped by a label making it a double entendre.

It may also be an extension of the 2Pac shoutout, as 2Pac famously beefed with all of Bad Boy Records in the 1990’s. Kendrick is making it clear he wants to sink Drake’s OVO Sound record label with the same passion.

lines.text: Put the wrong label on me, I'ma get 'em dropped, ayy
Sweet Chin Music and I won't pass the aux, ayy

annotations.text:
Kendrick Lamar makes reference to legendary WWE wrestler Shawn Michaels, whose signature finishing move is called “Sweet Chin Music.” The maneuver involves delivering a flat-footed kick to the opponent’s chin. Shawn Michaels used it to retire famous wrestler Ric Flair at Wrestlemania XXIV in 2008.

Shawn Michaels' greatest opponent was Canadian Bret Hart—both of whom had a legitimate feud—possibly mirroring the disdain Kendrick and Drake hold for each other.

 Shawn Michaels degrading a Canadian flag.

Furthermore, Shawn Michaels was famously part of the controversial 1997 “Montreal Screwjob” incident. Hart, who at the time held the WWE Championship, had agreed to depart WWE and join rival promotion WCW. Prior to the match, it was agreed that Hart would retain the title against Michaels in front of his hometown crowd, however, Michaels, WWE CEO Vince McMahon, and referee Earl Hebner conspired to “screw” Hart, and cost him the title.

Kendrick refusing to “pass the AUX”—an auxiliary cable commonly used to run audio from a device to a speaker system—coincides with Lamar’s barrage of diss tracks directed toward Drake within a 24-hour period: “6:16 in LA,” “meet the grahams,” and “Not Like Us.”

Amused by the back-and-forth, Micheals reacted to the lyric via Twitter/X:

lines.text: How many stocks do I really have in stock? Ayy

annotations.text:
Kendrick claims to have ten diss tracks. We’ve been exposed to five so far:



“Like That” — March 22

“euphoria” — April 30

“6:16 in LA” — May 3

“meet the grahams” — May 3
“Not Like Us” — May 4



With the exponentially decreasing time between drops, it’s not unlikely that Kendrick ends up fulfilling his promise, especially should the beef persist. This is also a callback to the earlier line that he can go further down the rabbit hole and expose everything he would have so far.

Kendrick also circles back to his previous mention of John Stockton:

How many stocks do I really have in stock [ten]

A “stock” is a defensive counting stat combining a player’s steals and blocks. The Hall of Fame point guard is also the NBA’s All-time leader in this category.

lines.text: One, two, three, four, five, plus five, ayy

Devil is a lie, he a 69 God, ayy
Freaky-ass niggas need to stay they ass inside, ayy

annotations.text:
The word “fan” doubles as an acronym for “freaky-ass nigga.” It can also be mentioned that Drake is being called a fan/spectator of the “black culture” rather than being a part of it. This is backed up by Kendrick calling Drake a colonizer, and not a colleague. While 69 is a well-known sex position. This is a jab at Drake, the self-proclaimed “6 God,” as well as an indirect reference to his sexual promiscuity; including rumors of pedophilia. It’s also possible that Kendrick is playing on the phonetic similarities between “69” and “16-eyeing.”

Drake previously flipped the iconic 6 to a 9 on his April 2016 release, “9”:

And I turn the six upside down, it’s a nine now

Kendrick may also be invoking rapper Tekashi 6ix9ine, who pled guilty to felony-level minor-related sex crimes which took place in 2015. He received a four-year probation period and a 1,000-hour community service order. Tekashi is widely considered to be a snitch because of his full cooperation with prosecutors and his testimony against former affiliates after being arrested on counts of racketeering and illegal firearms possession, and aiding in an attempted murder.

Kendrick could be making the same point he made on April 2024’s “euphoria,” when he name-dropped suspected-snitch and fellow rapper Gunna:

I know some shit about niggas that make Gunna Wunna look like a saint

Kendrick could also be playing off of Future’s Drake diss on March 2024’s “We Don’t Trust You”:

You a nigga number-one fan, dog

Drake himself flipped this line at the beginning of his own April 2024 diss track, “Push Ups”:

I could never be nobody number-one fan

lines.text: Roll they ass up like a fresh pack of 'za, ayy

annotations.text:
“Za” or “zaza” is slang for marijuana or cannabis. Kendrick suggests that he can easily “smoke” or eliminate his opponents, like rolling up a joint or a blunt.

Marijuana is commonly referenced within West Coast hip-hop. Here, even though Kendrick Lamar has commonly stated that he does not smoke weed or drink alcohol regularly, it’s time to boost the collective morale of every urban center from Los Angeles to The Bay and all the way to Atlanta.

lines.text: City is back up, it's a must, we outside, ayy
They not like us, they not like us, they not like us

annotations.text:
Kendrick comments on how Drake (and the people he surrounds himself with) are “not like us,” saying that anybody that Kendrick surrounds himself with would not replicate the behaviours that he refers to within the track, a contrast of pgLang and Top Dawg Entertainment with OVO Sound in terms of moral fibre, values and its ability to fit into Black American culture.

This theme of racial consideration has also likely been directed at Drake’s biracial background and the issues of acceptance that come with it, both from himself or from others. This subject has come up multiple times in Drake’s career, on his own tracks, such as November 2023’s “Wick Man”:

Black America love to remind me what my mama look like
As if I’d ever fuckin' forget
I’m never enough

And on other people’s songs too, such as Pusha T’s 2018 diss track “The Story of Adidon”:

Confused, always felt you weren’t Black enough
Afraid to grow it ‘cause your ‘fro wouldn’t nap enough

This can also be seen throughout the plethora of recent diss tracks between Drake and his opponents, including on Rick Ross’s “Champagne Moments”, where he would rap:

White boy, I see you, I see you

In reference to his White Jewish mother’s ethnic heritage and his suburban upbringing, linking back to the line with Kendrick pointing out that Drake’s childhood was unlike many of Black Americans that he attempts to fit in with. Alongside this, we can also analyse the track’s cover art, which sees an unknown White man’s yearbook photo that quickly became a meme in 2022 after it was identified that he comically resembled the Canadian rapper.



Above: the aforementioned cover art of Rick Ross's Champagne Moments


and Kendrick Lamar’s “euphoria”, on which he would state:

I even hate when you say the word “nigga,” but that’s just me, I guess
Some shit just cringeworthy, it ain’t even gotta be deep, I guess
[…]
We don’t wanna hear you say “nigga” no more
We don’t wanna hear you say “nigga” no more
Stop

To even further increase the gap between the two, highlighting how he feels uncomfortable with his usage of the N-word as he doesn’t acknowledge him as a Black person.

It is also important to note that Kendrick and Drake’s careers took off at around the same time. “They not like us” may allude to Kendrick’s commitment to authenticity and lyricism (the heart and soul of hip hop), in contrast to Drake’s catchy yet vapid songs. Here, Kendrick continues to differentiate himself from Drake not only on the basis of race, but also in terms of true commitment to the craft and its Black origins.

Finally, in “Kendrick Lamar’s Inner Drive,” a Lamar interview with SZA in the October 21, 2024 Harper’s Bazaar, SZA engages this song directly, asking “What does ‘Not Like Us’ mean to you?,” to which Lamar responds:

Not like us? Not like us is the energy of who I am, the type of man I represent. Now, if you identify with the man that I represent […] This man has morals, he has values, he believes in something, he stands on something. He’s not pandering. He’s a man who can recognize his mistakes and not be afraid to share the mistakes and can dig deep down into fear-based ideologies or experiences to be able to express them without feeling like he’s less of a man. If I’m thinking of ‘Not Like Us,’ I’m thinking of me and whoever identifies with that.

lines.text: They not like us, they not like us, they not like us

Once upon a time, all of us was in chains
The settlers was usin' townfolk to make 'em richer

annotations.text:
These lines are a response to Drake’s choice of words on May 2024’s “Family Matters”:

Always rappin’ like you ’bout to get the slaves freed
You just actin' like an activist, it’s make-believe

Drake also mentioned Atlanta in the same song:

Weeknd music gettin' played in all the spots where boys got a little more pride
That’s why all your friends dippin' to Atlanta, payin' just to find a tour guide

Kendrick references Atlanta as “The Black Mecca” here, a term that’s widely used for the city and steeped in black culture itself since the nickname was first used on the cover of Ebony magazine in 1971. Kendrick notes that it was already a “Mecca” of sorts as the centre of the slave trade where black people were forced to build “railroads and trains.” In modern times Atlanta has become a symbol of black excellence in art and culture, with every Atlanta mayor since the 1970’s being African American.

This is not the only time Drake has referred to enslaved African Americans this way. On “Slime You Out” from October 2023’s For All The Dogs, he says:

Whipped and chained you like American slaves

To “double down” is to double one’s wager in the game of  blackjack or to continue something with more intensity or determination. Kendrick uses this wordplay to provide further evidence of Drake’s complete disconnection from and disregard for Black culture.

This disconnection makes it impossible for him to understand the importance of Atlanta, Georgia as a Black Mecca or holy city, both then and now. Lamar likens Drake’s interest in collaborating with a plethora of African American artists from Atlanta to the exploitation perpetrated by “settlers” under slavery, with Drake’s interests being purely selfish—increasing his street credibility and earning more money rather than engaging the longer history of African American struggle across the South.

lines.text: Homie still doubled down callin' us some slaves
Atlanta was the Mecca, buildin' railroads and trains
Bear with me for a second, let me put y'all on game

annotations.text:
Kendrick Lamar might be alluding to The Game’s 2005 song “Put You on The Game,” as Drake mentioned Chuck Taylor—or Jayceon Terrell Taylor, The Game’s real name—on April 2024’s “Family Matters”:

You know who really bang a set? My nigga Chuck T

“Put You on the Game” was the first song that Game released after his split from G-Unit and before his long-lasting beef with 50 Cent. To be “put on game” is also to be taught important or useful information. Kendrick clarifies that his history lesson in the previous bars isn’t a tangent; it’s backstory for an important punchline against Drake that he’s setting up later in this verse when he says:

No, you not a colleague, you a fuckin' colonizer

Lastly, it’s worth noting that when Kendrick says “bear with me” it sounds similar to “bear Whitney”—which would be a subtle shout-out to his fiancée, Whitney Alford, who was mentioned on Drake’s other diss tracks.

lines.text: Fast-forward, 2024, you got the same agenda
You run to Atlanta when you need a check balance

annotations.text:
Kendrick claims Drake runs to Atlanta to collaborate with artists there, purely to make some money.

As Kendrick discusses in the upcoming lines, this is typically done to increase Drake’s street credibility and get him radio play in more urban locations, indirectly making him more money by exposing his brand to a different demographic.

This furthers the accusation that Drake is a culture vulture who exploits the Black community for financial gain. Drake sees himself as separate from them, only visiting and seeking their approval to profit from struggles he does not relate to.

lines.text: Let me break it down for you, this the real nigga challenge

annotations.text:
In 2018, Drake’s track “In My Feelings” was used for the mega-viral Kiki dance challenge, where people posted videos on social media of themselves jumping out of moving cars to dance to the song, set to the chorus where Drake is pining over a woman.

“The x challenge” is a popular phrase used in memes and to describe social media trends where users post videos of themselves doing the same, often outlandish, thing. Kendrick is accusing Drake of doing the “real nigga challenge” where he uses more legitimate hip hop artists to boost his popularity with black audiences and image of authenticity, as he lays out in the following bars. He’s contrasting the pop frivolity and marketing-focused nature of the Kiki challenge with Drake doing the “real nigga challenge” to feign authenticity in hip hop. He also goes on to bring up exactly 6 “real niggas” that Drake used, a reference to Drake calling himself the 6 god… or 69 god, as Kendrick says.

The Kiki challenge was criticized and even called out by police departments for being dangerous and illegal, and particularly out of concern for children and teens who are more likely to attempt reckless social media stunts. Kendrick is alluding to yet another way Drake has contributed to putting kids in danger.

This line also refers to how this track is set to a more danceable beat and has a short hook, unlike Kendrick’s previous disses, especially “meet the grahams”. In contrast to Drake’s soft song about Kiki, “Not Like Us” could be used for people to dance for the “real nigga challenge”. Kendrick is showing he can do what Drake does, make songs to dance to, and much more. Clips of DJs playing the song and crowds dancing following its Saturday evening release got attention on social media.

lines.text: You called Future when you didn't see the club (Ayy, what?)

annotations.text:
Kendrick claims Drake only collaborated with Future to get play in urban clubs.

Future and Drake’s first collaboration came in 2011, on track “Tony Montana”. Around this time, Drake faced criticism and skepticism from some hip-hop purists and fans who questioned his authenticity and street credibility due to his background as a child actor on “Degrassi: The Next Generation” and his more melodic, soft style compared to the harder-edged rap of some of his peers. This led to a mixed reception among traditional hip-hop circles, where debates about his place in the genre were common. Due to this regular discourse, Drake’s music was not regularly played in urban clubs, unlike Future’s. Kendrick claims this collaboration only manifested to foster Drake’s music into urban clubs.

Drake and Future have gone on to create a plethora of collaborations, most notably 2015’s collaboration mixtape What A Time To Be Alive.

Future’s dominant presence in the Atlanta club scene and underground is corroborated by fellow Atlanta artist 21 Savage, who said in an interview that most people in Atlanta believe Future is bigger than Drake:

In Atlanta, there’s a lot of niggas who you really can’t tell them that Future ain’t bigger than Drake. I used to think that…Drake ain’t really no big mixtape artist out there. A regular street nigga don’t give a fuck about how many times you went No.1, how many white people listen to your shit, because the farthest they think is the club

Future and Drake

lines.text: Lil Baby helped you get your lingo up (What?)

annotations.text:
Kendrick suggests that Drake collaborated and spent time with Lil Baby just to step his game up and that his usage of slang isn’t authentic. This is a doubling down on sentiments expressed in previous diss tracks, notably “euphoria,” where Kendrick argues that Drake has built a ‘gangster’ persona just to feel accepted in the industry and appear ‘black enough.’

How many more Black features ‘til you finally feel that you’re Black enough?

lines.text: 21 gave you false street cred

annotations.text:
Drake has an extensive history of collaboration with 21 Savage—someone who is considered a bonafide gangster rapper. They’ve release several song’s since Drake’s October 2016 track, “Sneakin',” and even shared a joint project together in November 2022, titled Her Loss.



This line could be seen as a criticism against Drake for propping up 21 Savage, but it may also suggest that Drake’s “street cred” or “tough guy” persona is grounded in his affiliation with those who have past gang affiliations.

lines.text: Thug made you feel like you a slime in your head (Ayy, what?)

annotations.text:
Thug has long been associated with slime, from lyrical references to rumors of Thug leading the Young Slime Street gang; Quavo sang about the Northside as a part of Migos; 2Chainz featured Drake on “No Lie.”

Although this may be a stretch, together, these lines could be telling a story. Through Young Thug, Drake believed he was, too, a “slime,” or a gang member. Quavo, however, encouraged Drake to embrace the Northside (AKA, the suburb where Drake grew up.) Irrespective of how Drake identifies, though, one thing is certain: Drake is not “good,“ or cool, or someone worth idolizing.

lines.text: Quavo said you can be from Northside (What?)
2 Chainz say you good, but he lied
You run to Atlanta when you need a few dollars

annotations.text: Every rapper mentioned in previous lines in the song is from Atlanta, Georgia. Drake is often criticized for being a culture vulture, and Atlanta is one of the epicenters of hip-hop culture. Kendrick suggests that when Drake needs another hit song or a new flow to steal, he goes to Atlanta and piggybacks off the talent that already exists there without adding anything new.

lines.text: No, you not a colleague, you a fuckin' colonizer

annotations.text:
The term “colonizer” identifies Drake’s relationship to hip hop and Black culture. Lamar uses the distinction between “colleague” and “colonizer” to indicate that Drake, who is half white, benefits from and appropriates Black culture without fully understanding the experiences and struggles of being Black. Similarly, Drake has long been accused of adopting accents inauthentically, appropriating Jamaican patois, for example, for his own financial benefit.

Drake being called a colonizer is currently a popular joke on X, where the collective response centers on Drake’s purchasing of “hip-hop heirlooms” in a similar fashion to British colonizers seizing and plundering artifacts abroad for their museums and exhibitions. Drake’s purchase of Tupac’s ring, a flash point in Lamar’s lyrics already, is but one example.
https://x.com/Ceepz/status/1786682319987675265

Further, the statement “you not a colleague” implies that Drake is not part of African American culture, continuing the theme of “Not Like Us.” This also clarifies the idea that Drake is not in “Cole’s League,” turning the notion that Drake is part of the Big 3 on its head, with “Cole’s League” being only that of Kendrick and Cole, with Drake excluded. This identifies both Kendrick and Cole as a part of the culture while placing Drake outside of it.

lines.text: The family matter and the truth of the matter

annotations.text:
“Family Matters” is Drake’s response to Kendrick’s two tracks: “euphoria” and “6:16 in LA.”

However “6:16 in LA,” “Family Matters” and “meet the grahams” were all published within a 24hr period.

Kendrick mentioning “Family Matters” on this track directly shows that this single is newly recorded, following “meet the grahams” release the day before.

Kendrick plays off “God’s Plan” with Drake’s 2018 track, implying all in all, God intended to show everyone Drake’s character as a liar, which he mentioned in “euphoria”:

Know you a master manipulator and habitual liar too
But don’t tell no lie about me and I won’t tell truths ‘bout you

All of this is worth noting, how Kendrick backs up his timing of dedication to exposing Drake’s affairs, which he foreshadows in “6:16 in LA”:

Often, I know this type of power is gon' cost
But I live in circadian rhythms of a shooting star

This song would go on to beat out “God’s Plan” for fastest hip hop song to reach 100 million Spotify streams.

lines.text: It was God's plan to show y'all the liar

Mm
Freaky-ass nigga, he a 69 God

annotations.text:
The word “fan” doubles as an acronym for “freaky-ass nigga.” It can also be mentioned that Drake is being called a fan/spectator of the “black culture” rather than being a part of it. This is backed up by Kendrick calling Drake a colonizer, and not a colleague. While 69 is a well-known sex position. This is a jab at Drake, the self-proclaimed “6 God,” as well as an indirect reference to his sexual promiscuity; including rumors of pedophilia. It’s also possible that Kendrick is playing on the phonetic similarities between “69” and “16-eyeing.”

Drake previously flipped the iconic 6 to a 9 on his April 2016 release, “9”:

And I turn the six upside down, it’s a nine now

Kendrick may also be invoking rapper Tekashi 6ix9ine, who pled guilty to felony-level minor-related sex crimes which took place in 2015. He received a four-year probation period and a 1,000-hour community service order. Tekashi is widely considered to be a snitch because of his full cooperation with prosecutors and his testimony against former affiliates after being arrested on counts of racketeering and illegal firearms possession, and aiding in an attempted murder.

Kendrick could be making the same point he made on April 2024’s “euphoria,” when he name-dropped suspected-snitch and fellow rapper Gunna:

I know some shit about niggas that make Gunna Wunna look like a saint

Kendrick could also be playing off of Future’s Drake diss on March 2024’s “We Don’t Trust You”:

You a nigga number-one fan, dog

Drake himself flipped this line at the beginning of his own April 2024 diss track, “Push Ups”:

I could never be nobody number-one fan

annotations.text:
“Run for Your Life” is a song by the English rock band the Beatles from their 1965 album Rubber Soul. The song’s lyrics establish a threatening tone towards the singer’s unnamed girlfriend, referred to throughout the song as “little girl.” It was also briefly banned by CFRA radio station in Ottawa for its threatening tone, deeming it to be misogynistic.

Another interpretation of the line could be Dot alluding to Bill Cosby’s “Fat Albert,” known for his catchphrase “Hey, Hey, Hey!” Bill Cosby has been accused by over 60 women of rape, drug-facilitated sexual assault, sexual battery, child sexual abuse, and sexual harassment.

annotations.text:
Kendrick Lamar mocks Drake’s brand and label, OVO or October’s Very Own, with a catchy call-and-response.

He originally referred to OVO as “OV-ho” on his first diss track directed at Drake, April 2024’s “euphoria”:

Yeah, OV-ho niggas is dick riders

Kendrick used similar wordplay on his third diss track, May 2024’s “meet the grahams”:

He got sex offenders on ho-VO that he keep on a monthly allowance

Nas also used this tactic on his December 2001 JAY-Z diss, “Ether,” when he mocked Jay’s label Roc-A-Fella Records:

That this Gay-Z and Cock-a-Fella Records wanted beef

lines.text: Say, "OV-ho" (OV-ho)
Then step this way, step that way

annotations.text:
Many fans believed Drake’s advantage in the two’s beef would be in creating a club-friendly diss track; few expected Kendrick Lamar to beat him to the punch. These simple dance instructions are reminiscent of Drake’s 2020 single, “Toosie Slide.” Kendrick may also be incorporating some homage to the title of his May 2022 album, Mr. Morale & The Big Steppers.

Once again, Kendrick alludes to their former friendship, somewhat facetiously, as he did on April 2024’s “euphoria.” After such an egregious exchange of insults and personal attacks, it’s unlikely Drake or Lamar will ever be on friendly terms again; this would be acknowledged later, on September 2024’s “Watch The Party Die”:

I think it’s time to watch the party die
This shit done got too wicked to apologize
